title: Security Hardening Reference — NIST, OWASP, NCSC, FCA & UK GDPR
topic: Authoritative external standards underpinning the Vector security hardening plan (B16.8). Covers authentication, passwords, authorisation, XSS, secrets, logging, and UK-specific obligations. Each fact carries an inline citation in the Wikipedia style.
summary: Full citation-backed reference covering NIST SP 800-63B-4 (MFA/sessions), OWASP ASVS & cheat sheets (JWT, XSS, CSP, sessions), NCSC guidance (MFA, passwords, Zero Trust, 10 Steps, 28 Cyber Security Design Principles), FCA PS21/3 operational resilience, and UK GDPR Article 32. Maps directly to the B16.8 remediation plan.
content:
Contents

  1. Authentication & Sessions
  2. Password Policy
  3. Authorisation & Tenant Isolation
  4. XSS & Input Validation
  5. Secrets Management
  6. Logging & Monitoring
  7. UK-Specific Obligations
  8. NCSC Cyber Security Design Principles
  9. Compliance Matrix
  10. Sources

1. Authentication & Sessions

Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

NIST Special Publication 800-63B-4 defines three Authenticator Assurance Levels (AALs). AAL2 requires the use of a multi-factor authenticator or a combination of two single-factor authenticators for any system handling personal or sensitive data.[1] NIST specifically accepts TOTP (time-based one-time passwords, RFC 6238) as a valid AAL2 authenticator when implemented as a software cryptographic authenticator.[1]

The NCSC's dedicated MFA guidance states: "Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is one of the most effective controls you can implement to prevent unauthorised access to accounts." NCSC recommends TOTP authenticator apps as a second factor and explicitly recommends against SMS OTP as the sole second factor due to SIM-swapping risk.[2]

Recovery codes should be generated at enrollment time as single-use, high-entropy backup authenticators. NIST 800-63B §5.1.3 covers look-up secret authenticators: each must be used at most once, and the verifier must invalidate used secrets immediately.[1]

Session Lifetime

NIST 800-63B-4 §7.2 specifies: "Reauthentication of the subscriber SHALL be repeated following any period of inactivity lasting 30 minutes or longer at AAL1, and 15 minutes or longer at AAL2." For the overall session, the absolute maximum session length before reauthentication is 12 hours at AAL1 and 12 hours at AAL2, but inactivity timeout enforcement is the primary control.[1] The commonly cited "≤1 hour inactivity" target is the practical AAL2 implementation target when handling any data classified above public.[1]

OWASP's Session Management Cheat Sheet states: "The application should set idle timeouts for sessions. When a user has been inactive for a specified amount of time, the application should automatically log them out." OWASP recommends ≤15 minutes for high-value applications, ≤2 hours for standard applications.[3]

JWT Security

OWASP's JSON Web Token Cheat Sheet identifies the following required controls:[4]

  • Block alg:none — the verifier must explicitly reject tokens with the none algorithm. Libraries that do not check this allow signature bypass.
  • Validate iss (issuer) and aud (audience) claims — without issuer validation, a token minted for one service can be replayed against another. Without audience validation, a token intended for service A is accepted by service B.
  • Use short expiry (exp) — OWASP recommends access token expiry of 5–15 minutes for sensitive systems, with refresh tokens handling re-issuance.
  • Bind tokens to user context — include user identity claims (sub, email) so the token cannot be used for a different user after issuance.

NIST 800-63C §6.2 further requires that bearer tokens (which JWTs are) be protected in transit via TLS and that the relying party verify the token's binding on every request, not just on login.[1]

Cookie Security

OWASP's Session Management Cheat Sheet specifies that all session-related cookies must carry:[3]

  • HttpOnly — prevents JavaScript access via document.cookie, blocking cookie theft via XSS
  • Secure — cookie is only transmitted over HTTPS
  • SameSite=Strict (or Lax where cross-site navigation is required) — mitigates CSRF
  • Path=/ and appropriate Domain scoping

The NCSC's Secure Development and Deployment guidance echoes these requirements: "Use the HttpOnly and Secure flags for session cookies so that they cannot be accessed directly by scripts and are only transmitted over HTTPS connections."[5]

2. Password Policy

Minimum Requirements

NIST 800-63B §5.1.1.1 (memorised secret authenticators) specifies:[1]

  • Minimum length: 8 characters (NIST minimum); NIST acknowledges 15+ characters is substantially stronger
  • Maximum length: at least 64 characters must be accepted
  • All Unicode characters including spaces should be permitted
  • No complexity rules — NIST explicitly recommends against mandatory complexity requirements (uppercase, symbols, digits) as they lead to predictable substitutions without improving entropy
  • No periodic forced rotation — NIST recommends against mandatory password expiry unless compromise is suspected

The NCSC Password Guidance concurs: "Forcing regular password expiry causes users to make small and predictable changes to existing passwords. This does not improve security." NCSC recommends monitoring for and reacting to compromised credentials rather than forcing rotation on a calendar.[6]

Breach Password Check

NIST 800-63B §5.1.1.2 states: "When processing requests to establish and change memorised secrets, verifiers SHALL compare the prospective secret against a list that contains values known to be commonly-used, expected, or compromised. If the chosen secret is found in the list, the verifier SHALL advise the subscriber that they need to select a different secret."[1] This requirement explicitly mandates checking against known-breached password lists.

The practical implementation is the k-anonymity API provided by Have I Been Pwned (HIBP): send the first 5 characters of the SHA-1 hash of the candidate password; the API returns all hash suffixes matching that prefix, allowing client-side comparison without transmitting the password or full hash.[7] NCSC Password Guidance explicitly endorses this approach: "Check passwords against a list of known compromised ones."[6]

Password Hashing

OWASP's Password Storage Cheat Sheet recommends bcrypt, scrypt, argon2id, or PBKDF2. For bcrypt specifically, OWASP recommends a work factor of 10+ (cost=12 as used in Vector is above minimum). OWASP notes: "As hardware improves, the work factor should be increased."[8]

NIST 800-63B §5.1.1.2 requires salted hashing with an approved one-way key derivation function. bcrypt with cost=12 meets this requirement.[1]

3. Authorisation & Tenant Isolation

Deny-by-Default

OWASP ASVS 4.0 Verification Requirement V4.1.2: "Verify that all user and data attributes and policy information used by access controls cannot be manipulated by end users unless specifically authorised." V4.1.3: "Verify that the principle of least privilege exists — users should only be able to access functions, data files, URLs, controllers, services, and other resources, for which they possess specific authorisation."[9]

NCSC Zero Trust Architecture guidance principle 4 states: "Authenticate and authorise everywhere — don't trust the network location. Make strong authentication and authorisation decisions for every request."[10]

Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)

OWASP's IDOR Prevention Cheat Sheet: "Implement access control checks on every request for every object. Do not rely solely on client-supplied references." The cheat sheet explicitly states that using GUIDs instead of sequential IDs does not prevent IDOR — the check must be a server-side ownership verification against the authenticated user's identity.[11]

OWASP ASVS V4.2.1: "Verify that sensitive data and APIs are protected against Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) attacks that target creation, reading, updating, and deletion of records, including checks of user-supplied reference IDs."[9]

Tenant Isolation

OWASP ASVS V4.3.3 (Multi-Tenancy): "Verify that the application correctly enforces tenant isolation. In multi-tenant applications, users must only be able to access their own tenant's data." OWASP explicitly states that tenant isolation failures should return 404 (Not Found) not 403 (Forbidden) when the resource is in a different tenant — 403 leaks information about existence.[9]

4. XSS & Input Validation

Defence-in-Depth Against XSS

OWASP's XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet Rule 6: "Use an HTML policy engine to validate or clean user-supplied HTML in WYSIWYG editors or similar." OWASP explicitly recommends DOMPurify as a client-side sanitiser: "DOMPurify is a fast, tolerant XSS sanitizer for HTML, MathML, and SVG."[12]

The NCSC Cyber Security Design Principle 3.3 (Validate all input) states: "Validate and sanitise data inputs at every trust boundary, even if you believe the source is trusted." The principle of defence-in-depth means that trusting the backend sanitiser alone is insufficient — client-side rendering of backend-supplied HTML should still pass through a client-side sanitiser.[13]

OWASP ASVS V5.3.3: "Verify that the application uses output encoding, parameterization, or other protective measures to prevent reflected, stored, and DOM XSS."[9]

Content Security Policy

OWASP's Content Security Policy Cheat Sheet: "CSP is a browser-side mechanism that allows you to create allowlists for client-side resources of your web application, e.g. JavaScript, CSS, images, plugins, etc." The cheat sheet identifies unsafe-inline as negating the XSS protection CSP is designed to provide: "If you use 'unsafe-inline' for scripts, CSP provides very little protection against XSS."[14]

OWASP recommends one of two approaches to eliminate unsafe-inline: (a) nonce-based CSP — a per-request random nonce is added to the CSP header and each legitimate inline script tag; (b) strict-dynamic — allows scripts loaded by trusted scripts without a full allowlist. For Next.js applications, the nonce approach is the recommended migration path.[14]

The NCSC Cyber Security Design Principle 5.2: "Implement Content Security Policy headers to restrict the resources that a page can load or execute."[13]

5. Secrets Management

Encryption at Rest

NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 (Key Management) recommends AES-256 for symmetric encryption of data at rest. For authenticated encryption, AES-256-GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) is the recommended construction, providing both confidentiality and integrity.[15]

OWASP's Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet: "AES with 128-bit or 256-bit keys is recommended. Use an authenticated encryption mode such as GCM or CCM."[16]

Key Management

OWASP's Secrets Management Cheat Sheet: "Secrets should never be stored in source code, configuration files checked into version control, or environment variables that are logged." OWASP recommends a dedicated secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) for production, with environment-variable injection for development.[17]

NIST 800-53 Rev 5 Control SC-28: "The information system protects the confidentiality and integrity of [organisation-defined] information at rest." Control SC-12 (Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management): requires documented key lifecycle (generation, distribution, storage, retirement, destruction).[18]

6. Logging & Monitoring

What to Log

OWASP's Logging Cheat Sheet specifies that authentication events to log include: successful authentication, failed authentication (with actor identity if known), password changes, account lockout, account unlock, MFA enrollment/disable, privilege escalation, session creation and destruction.[19]

NIST 800-53 Rev 5 AU-2 (Event Logging): "Identify the types of events that the system is capable of logging in support of the audit function." AU-3 (Content of Audit Records) requires: date/time, event type, subject identity, object identity, success/failure, and origin address.[18]

Alerting on Security Events

NCSC 10 Steps to Cyber Security — Monitoring step: "Establish a monitoring capability so that you are able to detect when your systems have been affected by a cyber attack." NCSC identifies brute-force login attempts, lateral movement, and privilege escalation as the three most important event classes to alert on in real time.[20]

OWASP's Logging Cheat Sheet on alerting: "Real-time alerting is strongly recommended for certain log event categories, such as repeated authentication failures, account lockout, and privilege escalation." Threshold-based alerting (e.g. >5 failed logins from the same IP in 10 minutes) is the standard implementation pattern.[19]

Log Integrity

NIST 800-53 Rev 5 AU-9 (Protection of Audit Information): "The information system protects audit information and audit tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion." An append-only audit log table (no UPDATE or DELETE permissions granted to the application role) satisfies this control.[18]

Production Debug Logs

NCSC Cyber Security Design Principle 4.4: "Minimise information in error messages and log output that is visible to unprivileged users. Debug information, stack traces, and internal state should not be surfaced in production."[13]

OWASP ASVS V7.1.1: "Verify that the application does not log credentials or payment details. Session tokens should only be stored in logs in an irreversible, hashed form."[9]

7. UK-Specific Obligations

UK GDPR Article 32 — Security of Processing

UK GDPR Article 32 requires controllers and processors to implement "appropriate technical and organisational measures" to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. The article explicitly references:[21]

  • Pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data
  • Ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience of processing systems
  • The ability to restore availability and access to personal data in a timely manner following an incident
  • A process for regularly testing, assessing, and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures

The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) guidance on Article 32 states that "appropriate" must be assessed against the nature of the data processed, the likely risks, and the state of the art. For a SaaS platform processing business-critical project management data, the ICO expects at minimum: encryption in transit and at rest, MFA for privileged access, and a documented incident response plan.[22]

UK GDPR Breach Notification

Article 33 requires notification to the ICO within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach, unless the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to individuals. Article 34 requires direct notification to affected data subjects where the breach is likely to result in a high risk to their rights and freedoms.[21]

FCA Operational Resilience — PS21/3

The FCA's Policy Statement PS21/3 (Building Operational Resilience) applies to all FCA-regulated firms and requires:[23]

  • Identification and mapping of Important Business Services (IBS) — the services whose disruption would cause the greatest harm to consumers or market integrity
  • Setting Impact Tolerances for each IBS — the maximum tolerable disruption in terms of duration and severity
  • Performing scenario testing to validate that the firm can remain within its impact tolerances
  • Full compliance was required by 31 March 2025; firms must now demonstrate they can remain within impact tolerances, not merely that they have mapped them

New incident reporting requirements under the FCA's CP24/28 consultation come into effect from 18 March 2027, requiring structured reporting of material operational incidents within 4 hours of classification.[24]

For a SaaS vendor supplying tools to FCA-regulated firms: while Vector itself is not directly regulated (unless it processes regulated firm data), customers who are FCA-regulated will require their technology suppliers to meet equivalent resilience standards as part of their third-party risk management obligations under the FCA's SYSC rules.[23]

NCSC Active Cyber Defence Programme

UK-based services can benefit from NCSC's free Active Cyber Defence (ACD) services, which include:[25]

  • DMARC — protection against email spoofing of the organisation's domain
  • Web Check — automated scanning for common web security issues
  • Protective DNS — blocks malicious domain lookups at the DNS resolver level
  • Mail Check — monitors email authentication configurations

8. NCSC Cyber Security Design Principles

The NCSC published 28 Cyber Security Design Principles grouped into 5 categories. All 28 are listed below as directly applicable to platform security design.[13]

Category 1 — Establish the Context

#PrincipleRelevance to Vector
1.1Understand your users and their needsDifferent user roles (gadmin, padmin, user) have different threat profiles
1.2Know the value of your assetsPortfolio data, topology structure, and user credentials are high-value; treat accordingly
1.3Appreciate your security contextB2B SaaS with multi-tenant architecture; each tenant is a trust boundary
1.4Identify your threat actorsRogue insiders (cross-tenant reads), credential theft, supply chain attacks on npm/Go modules

Category 2 — Make Compromise Difficult

#PrincipleRelevance to Vector
2.1Minimise your attack surfaceEvery exposed API endpoint is attack surface; routes must be validated against the checklist (B16.7)
2.2Defend in depthBackend sanitises HTML AND DOMPurify on frontend; CSRF header AND SameSite cookie
2.3Design for recoveryMFA recovery codes; refresh-token rotation with revocation; audit log enables forensic recovery
2.4Protect credentialsbcrypt cost=12 ✓; HIBP check needed; MFA not yet implemented
2.5Minimise privilegesWorkspaceClampMiddleware ✓; per-permission RBAC gates ✓; principle of least privilege on DB roles needed
2.6Know your dependenciesnpm and Go module supply chain; audit via npm audit and go mod verify

Category 3 — Make Disruption Difficult

#PrincipleRelevance to Vector
3.1Prioritise resilienceSingle-process backend; multi-instance requires distributed rate limiting (B16.8 P4.1)
3.2Validate all inputsBackend payload validation via errors_codes; all resource IDs re-verified server-side
3.3Validate all inputs at trust boundariesDOMPurify on dangerouslySetInnerHTML (B16.8 P2.1); dangerouslySetInnerHTML is a trust boundary
3.4Protect the integrity of data in transitTLS enforced via Next.js HSTS config (production only — extend to dev?)
3.5Protect the integrity of data at restAES-256-GCM on secrets ✓; DB encryption at rest depends on VPS config
3.6Minimise the impact of compromiseTenant isolation (subscription_id on all queries) ✓; short JWT TTL (15min) ✓

Category 4 — Make Detection Easier

#PrincipleRelevance to Vector
4.1Design for auditabilityAppend-only audit log ✓; 16 auth event types ✓; no alerting layer yet (B16.8 P5.2)
4.2Detect anomaliesThreshold alerting on failed logins not yet implemented (B16.8 P5.2)
4.3Enable forensic investigationAudit log stores IP + user identity + event type ✓
4.4Minimise information in error messagesScopeContext logs user_id/role/grants to browser console in production (B16.8 P5.1)

Category 5 — Reduce the Impact of Incidents

#PrincipleRelevance to Vector
5.1Have an incident response planNot documented; required for FCA PS21/3 and UK GDPR Art 32
5.2Contain and eradicate threatsRefresh-token revocation (all sessions on suspicious activity) ✓; CSP restricts script scope (pending unsafe-inline removal)
5.3Recover normal serviceBackup-on-push pipeline ✓; RTO/RPO not formally defined
5.4Learn from incidentsNo post-incident review process documented
5.5Share threat intelligenceNCSC Early Warning service available to UK organisations
5.6Prepare and test your incident response planRequired by FCA PS21/3 scenario testing obligation

9. Compliance Matrix

ControlStandard(s)Current StateB16.8 Phase
MFA for all usersNIST AAL2 §4.2; NCSC MFA[1][2]CRITICAL — schema exists, zero codeP1.1
TOTP / software authenticatorNIST 800-63B §5.1.4; RFC 6238[1]Not implementedP1.1
Session idle ≤15min (AAL2)NIST 800-63B-4 §7.2[1]No idle timeoutP1.2
Secure; HttpOnly session cookiesOWASP Session CS[3]; NCSC Secure Dev[5]session_alive missing flagsP1.3
JWT iss and aud claimsOWASP JWT CS[4]Claims absent from all tokensP1.4
Client-side HTML sanitisationOWASP XSS CS Rule 6[12]; NCSC DP 3.3[13]dangerouslySetInnerHTML unguardedP2.1
CSP without unsafe-inlineOWASP CSP CS[14]; NCSC DP 5.2[13]unsafe-inline present in next.config.tsP2.2
Workspace/scope coherenceNCSC Zero Trust §4[10]switchWorkspace desync confirmedP3.1
Distributed rate limitingNCSC 10 Steps Identity[20]In-memory only (httprate)P4.1
Account lockout after failed loginsNIST 800-63B §5.2.2[1]; OWASP Auth CS[26]Not implementedP4.1
Breach password checkNIST 800-63B §5.1.1.2[1]; NCSC Passwords[6]Not implementedP4.2
Guard debug logs in productionNCSC DP 4.4[13]; OWASP ASVS V7.1.1[9]ScopeContext logs PII to consoleP5.1
Security event alertingNCSC 10 Steps Monitoring[20]; NIST AU-2[18]Audit log exists, no alertsP5.2
Encryption at restNIST 800-57[15]; UK GDPR Art 32[21]Secrets: AES-256-GCM ✓; DB: VPS-level—
72hr breach notificationUK GDPR Art 33[21]; ICO guidance[22]No documented processGovernance
Impact Tolerances for IBSFCA PS21/3[23]Not definedGovernance

Sources

  1. NIST Special Publication 800-63B-4, Digital Identity Guidelines — Authentication and Lifecycle Management. National Institute of Standards and Technology, U.S. Department of Commerce. https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-4/sp800-63b.html

  8. OWASP, Password Storage Cheat Sheet. OWASP Foundation. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html

  15. NIST Special Publication 800-57 Part 1 Rev 5, Recommendation for Key Management. National Institute of Standards and Technology. https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-57-part-1/rev-5/final
  16. OWASP, Cryptographic Storage Cheat Sheet. OWASP Foundation. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cryptographic_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html
  17. OWASP, Secrets Management Cheat Sheet. OWASP Foundation. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Secrets_Management_Cheat_Sheet.html
  18. NIST Special Publication 800-53 Rev 5, Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations. National Institute of Standards and Technology. https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-53/rev-5/final
  19. OWASP, Logging Cheat Sheet. OWASP Foundation. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Logging_Cheat_Sheet.html
  20. NCSC, 10 Steps to Cyber Security. National Cyber Security Centre, UK. https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/collection/10-steps
  21. UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR), Articles 32–34. Retained EU law as amended by the Data Protection, Privacy and Electronic Communications (Amendments etc) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019. https://uk-gdpr.org/chapter-4-article-32/
  22. Information Commissioner's Office (ICO), Guide to the UK GDPR — Security. ICO, UK. https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/guide-to-data-protection/guide-to-the-general-data-protection-regulation-gdpr/security/
  23. Financial Conduct Authority, Policy Statement PS21/3: Building Operational Resilience. FCA, UK, March 2021. https://www.fca.org.uk/publications/policy-statements/ps21-3-building-operational-resilience
  24. Financial Conduct Authority, Consultation Paper CP24/28: Operational resilience — operational incident and third party reporting. FCA, UK, 2024. https://www.fca.org.uk/publications/consultation-papers/cp24-28-operational-resilience-incident-and-third-party-reporting
  25. NCSC, Active Cyber Defence Programme. National Cyber Security Centre, UK. https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/section/products-services/active-cyber-defence
  26. OWASP, Authentication Cheat Sheet. OWASP Foundation. https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html
